summary: O'Neil draws a parallel between the 2008 financial crisis and the current cryptocurrency bubble, arguing that both involve government protection of markets for political reasons. She recounts her early skepticism of Bitcoin evangelists at Occupy Wall Street who couldn't explain how crypto would democratize banking. The post's central concern is that Trump and his family are so heavily invested in crypto that they have personal financial incentives to prevent a market crash, creating a more direct and troubling conflict of interest than even the Obama-era bailouts.
key_points: Bitcoin evangelists in 2012 couldn't explain how blockchain would make banking more democratic, only pushed adoption
The replacement of pensions with stock portfolios created political pressure to never let markets fail, distorting natural corrections
The Tea Party blamed borrowers (especially people of color) for the 2008 crisis rather than the system that enabled it
Trump's family has billions personally invested in crypto, creating unprecedented conflicts of interest
Government intervention to protect bubbles rewards the wrong people while victims bear the costs
money_quotes: Come back when you can get rewarded for saving energy instead of wasting it.
It seemed outrageous that nobody was losing their shirt except for the victims of the whole scam, because it was political suicide to allow the markets to collapse.
As pensions were replaced by stock portfolios, it became a requirement to never allow stocks to dip too low for too long. And the result was truly non-natural and perverted.
This time it's Trump and his actual family that is so heavily invested in crypto that they personally stand to lose billions of dollars if and when the bubble bursts.
Which leads me to wonder, what will they end up doing to prevent this particular bubble from bursting? I don't know, but I'm guessing it will be really gross.
key_insight: When political leaders have personal billions at stake in a financial bubble, the inevitable government intervention to prevent its collapse will be even more corrupt than past bailouts.
